facts.text: The Broken Spoke opened in 1964 on South Lamar, founded by James and Annetta White. Austin's oldest continually operating honky-tonk.
Texas two-step evolved from the collegiate foxtrot of the 1920s, simplified for crowded Texas dance halls.
Willie Nelson was born in Abbott, TX (pop. ~350) and taught Sunday school at Fort Worth's Metropolitan Baptist Church alongside his early honky-tonk gigs.
"Luckenbach, Texas" (Waylon, 1977) was written by Chips Moman and Bobby Emmons — neither had ever been to Luckenbach.
Gruene Hall (New Braunfels, 1878) is the oldest continuously operating dance hall in Texas.

The Lindy Hop's "swing-out" — ancestor to many country-swing moves — was born at a 1928 Harlem dance marathon when George "Shorty" Snowden broke from closed position. The dance's name came from Charles Lindbergh's 1927 transatlantic "hop."
Urban Cowboy (1980), starring John Travolta, was based on Aaron Latham's 1978 Esquire article "The Ballad of the Urban Cowboy" about Gilley's in Pasadena, TX.
Armadillo World Headquarters (1970–1980) fused hippies and cowboys, launching the "Cosmic Cowboy" scene.
Urban Cowboy (1980), set at Gilley's in Pasadena, TX, sparked a nationwide two-step boom.
Bob Wills, "King of Western Swing," integrated horns and jazz into country in the 1930s — the foundation of most Texas dance-hall music.
"Deep in the Heart of Texas" was written in 1941 by June Hershey (born in Los Angeles) and Don Swander (Iowa-born, LA-based).
Luckenbach Dance Hall (1887) has a population of 3 — the whole town operates as a music and dance venue.
The "cuddle" / "sweetheart" position comes from Lindy Hop and East Coast Swing and was later adopted into country partner dance.
Pure Country (1992) was George Strait's only leading film role — the soundtrack is 6x platinum, his biggest-selling album.
Austin City Limits TV taped its pilot with Willie Nelson on October 17, 1974 and premiered on PBS in 1975.
Two-steppers always travel counterclockwise around the floor — the "line of dance" convention borrowed from European ballroom.
George Strait nearly quit music around 1980 for a cattle-pen design job in Uvalde — while living in San Marcos and attending SWT.
"London Homesick Blues" by Gary P. Nunn became Austin's anthem via Jerry Jeff Walker's 1973 recording on ¡Viva Terlingua!; it was the Austin City Limits theme from 1977 to 2004.
The Broken Spoke (1964) has a famously low ceiling — so low that many musicians can't wear cowboy hats on stage.
The word "polka" likely comes from the Czech "půlka" ("half") — referring to the dance's half-step and 2/4 meter. It spread from Bohemia to Prague by 1835 and Paris by 1840.
Yellowstone (2018–) triggered the current "Western revival" — cowboy hat sales at makers like Stetson and American Hat Co. rose sharply after the show premiered.
Austin officially adopted the slogan "Live Music Capital of the World" on August 29, 1991 — the claim based on a 1985 per-capita venue-count survey.
The standard two-step rhythm is "quick-quick-slow-slow" — some instructors teach it as "slow-slow-quick-quick."
Waylon Jennings was Buddy Holly's bassist on the 1959 Winter Dance Party tour and gave up his plane seat to J.P. Richardson ("The Big Bopper"); the plane crashed on Feb 3, 1959.
"All My Ex's Live in Texas" (George Strait, 1987) was written by husband-and-wife team Sanger D. Shafer and Lyndia J. Shafer.
Coupland Dance Hall's 1904 building originally housed a drugstore, hardware store, newspaper, and tavern — it wasn't converted into a dance hall until 1992.
The Texas Tommy was born around 1909 in San Francisco's Barbary Coast and actually predates Lindy Hop — its swing-out pattern helped seed Lindy's breakaway move.
Dazed and Confused (1993), filmed in Austin, is set on the last day of school in May 1976 — capturing late-70s Texas teen life.
The White Horse opened on Comal Street in East Austin in December 2011 and revived two-step dancing among younger Austinites.
The "triple two-step" (or "Fort Worth Shuffle") uses triple-triple-walk-walk footwork and is strongly associated with Fort Worth and North Texas.
Townes Van Zandt wrote "Pancho and Lefty," later made famous by Willie Nelson and Merle Haggard's 1983 duet — the only #1 country hit for either of Townes's most-covered songs.
"The Yellow Rose of Texas" first appeared in print in 1853 in Christy's Plantation Melodies No. 2; a 20th-century theory links the song to Emily D. West.
Many historic Texas dance halls were built by German and Czech immigrant societies — SPJST (Czech) and Turnverein and Schützen Verein (German) being the most common.

The first aerial ("air step") in a swing dance competition is credited to Frankie Manning and Frieda Washington in 1935, who executed a back-to-back flip against George Snowden's team at Harlem's Savoy Ballroom.
Austin City Limits Festival (ACL Fest) launched in 2002 in Zilker Park, inspired by the long-running PBS TV series.
Willie Nelson's Fourth of July Picnic started in 1973 in Dripping Springs and drew crowds estimated at 40,000–50,000 fans.
The "cowboy waltz" in 3/4 time is traditionally programmed alongside two-steps in Texas dance halls — a 1913 Elks dance card in central Texas documents the alternation.
Stevie Ray Vaughan was catapulted to international fame when David Bowie, after seeing him at the 1982 Montreux Jazz Festival, hired him for the Let's Dance album (1983).
"Amarillo by Morning" was written by Terry Stafford and Paul Fraser in 1973 after Stafford drove home from a San Antonio rodeo; George Strait's 1983 version made it famous.
Sengelmann Hall in Schulenburg (rebuilt 1894 after a fire) was restored and reopened in 2009 after a $2M preservation project — earning a National Trust Honor Award in 2010.
The Collegiate Shag originated in African-American communities of the Carolinas in the 1920s — danced to uptempo jazz in the 185–250+ BPM range, with a slow-slow-quick-quick step-and-hop pattern.
Tender Mercies (1983), with Robert Duvall as a country singer, won Best Actor for Duvall and Best Original Screenplay for Horton Foote at the Oscars.
Jerry Jeff Walker recorded ¡Viva Terlingua! live at Luckenbach Dance Hall in August 1973 — a seminal outlaw/progressive country album.
The polka is closely related to the two-step — sometimes described as "a two-step with a hop" — and most Texas dance halls alternate polkas and two-steps throughout a night.
Kris Kristofferson famously landed a National Guard helicopter on Johnny Cash's Hendersonville lawn in 1969 to drop off demo tapes — though Cash wasn't home at the time.
"Crazy" (Patsy Cline, 1961) was written by a ~27-year-old Willie Nelson; Cline reportedly struggled with Nelson's talk-style phrasing on the demo but recorded her own version.
Anhalt Hall (1875) hosts events for the Germania Farmer Verein — one of the oldest continuously active German-speaking organizations in Texas, with an Oktoberfest tradition dating to 1877.
The leader's right hand in closed position is placed on the follower's left shoulder blade in American social dance — standardized in ballroom manuals and carried into country partner dance.
Songwriter (1984) paired Willie Nelson and Kris Kristofferson as a country songwriting duo — a cult classic that featured songs by both men.
The "Cosmic Cowboy" movement was named after Michael Murphey's 1973 song from his Cosmic Cowboy Souvenir album.
The "schottische" — a hopping partner dance with Bohemian (Czech) origins — is still danced in some traditional Hill Country halls like Twin Sisters.

Guy Clark, the Texas songwriter legend, grew up in the Houston folk scene alongside Townes Van Zandt, Mickey Newbury, and Jerry Jeff Walker — then mentored younger writers like Rodney Crowell and Steve Earle in Nashville in the 1970s.

The phrase "friends in low places" was coined at a lunch at Nashville's Tavern on the Row when Earl Bud Lee forgot his wallet — Lee and Dewayne Blackwell wrote the actual song later on napkins at a party.
Hye Post Office (1904) is where a four-year-old Lyndon Johnson mailed his first letter; as president, LBJ swore in Postmaster General Lawrence O'Brien on its porch in 1965.
"Shadow" position (both partners facing the same direction) exists across ballroom, Latin, and country syllabi — Cowboy Cha-Cha popularized it in country dancing but didn't introduce it.
Coal Miner's Daughter (1980), the Loretta Lynn biopic, earned Sissy Spacek the Best Actress Oscar at the 53rd Academy Awards.
Threadgill's was a former Gulf filling station that Kenneth Threadgill converted in 1933; Janis Joplin performed there regularly with the Waller Creek Boys in 1962–1963.
Texas two-step is classified as a "progressive" or "traveling" partner dance — moving counterclockwise around the floor — which distinguishes it from spot-based line dances.
Roy Orbison was born in Vernon, TX in 1936 and grew up singing with his band the Wink Westerners at his high school in Wink, TX.
"The Dance" was written by Texas songwriter Tony Arata; Garth Brooks committed to recording it after hearing Arata perform it at Nashville's Douglas Corner Cafe.
Twin Sisters Dance Hall (opened January 1870 near Blanco) still uses its original 1870s hardwood floor, maintained with an oil-and-sawdust treatment.
"Wrap," "cuddle," "sweetheart," and "cradle" are all used interchangeably across different dance traditions — distinctions between them are often studio- or region-specific.
The Alamo (2004), filmed on a 51-acre ranch near Austin (Reimers Ranch area), starred Dennis Quaid as Sam Houston — and was a notorious box-office flop at $25.8M on a $107M budget.
SXSW launched as a music festival in March 1987 and added film and interactive tracks in 1994.
Line dancing boomed in the 1990s with Billy Ray Cyrus's "Achy Breaky Heart" (1992).

Freddy Fender (born Baldemar Garza Huerta in San Benito, TX) became the first major Mexican-American country crossover star when "Before the Next Teardrop Falls" topped both pop and country charts in 1975.
"Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain" was a 1945 Fred Rose song; Willie Nelson's 1975 version on Red Headed Stranger was his first #1 hit as a solo singer.
Cherry Springs Dance Hall hosted Elvis Presley on October 9, 1955 — the venue misspelled his name as "Clovis Presley" on the bill.

The story that West Coast Swing's narrow "slot" was invented for Hollywood cameras is considered folklore — wide-angle lenses with sufficient depth of field already existed in the 1920s, and 1940s films show dancers turning their backs to the camera.
Walker, Texas Ranger (1993–2001) ran for eight seasons on CBS — Chuck Norris played Texas Ranger Cordell Walker.
Austin City Limits Festival (ACL Fest) launched in September 2002 in Zilker Park.

"Cotton-Eyed Joe" as a song dates to before 1861, originating among enslaved African Americans in the South; the group dance familiar today evolved later with Irish/Appalachian and polka-like elements.
Lyle Lovett earned a dual degree in German and Journalism at Texas A&M in 1979 and wrote for The Battalion before his songwriting career took off.
"Mammas Don't Let Your Babies Grow Up to Be Cowboys" was written by Ed Bruce and his wife Patsy Bruce in 1975, then became a #1 hit for Willie Nelson & Waylon Jennings in 1978.
Quihi Gun Club & Dance Hall (1890) is the oldest continuously operating gun club in the United States and was raised on five-foot posts after repeated flooding.
West Coast Swing became the official state dance of California on October 1, 1988, via California Government Code §421.5 — square dance is separately designated the state folk dance.
Friday Night Lights (film 2004, TV series 2006–2011) were both based on Buzz Bissinger's 1990 book about the 1988 Permian Panthers high-school football team in Odessa, TX.
Asleep at the Wheel formed in Paw Paw, West Virginia in 1970, relocated to the Bay Area, and moved to Austin in 1974 at Willie Nelson's invitation — becoming a pillar of Western swing.
The "Tush Push," choreographed by Jim Ferrazzano around 1980, is one of the oldest and most enduring modern country line dances.
Buddy Holly was born Charles Hardin Holley in Lubbock in 1936; the "e" was dropped after a 1956 Decca contract misspelled his surname — his gravestone still reads "Holley."
"Neon Moon" by Brooks & Dunn was released in February 1992 on their Brand New Man album and became a defining Texas two-step song of the 1990s.
Fischer Hall near Wimberley (c. 1895, originally a schoolhouse) is an enclosed wood-frame community hall with hand-hewn beams — featured in Willie Nelson's Honeysuckle Rose.
"Sugar push" is a foundational West Coast Swing figure that originated in California around 1952 — it's one of WCS's four core patterns alongside the side pass, whip, and starter step.
Hell or High Water (2016) is set in West Texas but was filmed in eastern New Mexico (Clovis, Portales, Tucumcari); Jeff Bridges plays Texas Ranger Marcus Hamilton.
Luckenbach was bought in 1970 by Hondo Crouch, Guich Koock, and Kathy Morgan for $30,000 — the original for-sale ad listed "pop. 3."
German and Czech immigrants arrived in central Texas in the 1840s–50s, bringing the waltz and polka that shaped Texas dance-hall repertoire.
Janis Joplin's first band, the Waller Creek Boys (with Powell St. John and Lanny Wiggins), formed in 1962 and played the Texas Union and Threadgill's while she was at UT Austin.
"Desperados Waiting for a Train" (Guy Clark, 1975) was written about Jack Prigg — the longtime companion of Clark's grandmother and a grandfather figure to him in Monahans, TX.
Schroeder Hall near Goliad was built in 1935 with a 6,000-square-foot oak dance floor — men paid fifty cents to enter while women and children got in free.
Positions often have multiple names depending on instructor lineage — "wrap," "cuddle," and "sweetheart" are used interchangeably in most country social-dance contexts.
King of the Hill (1997–2009) is set in the fictional Arlen, TX — an amalgamation of Dallas suburbs Mike Judge drew from, especially Richardson, where he lived.
The Continental Club on South Congress opened in 1955 as a swanky supper club, founded by Morin Scott.
Partner dancing in Texas halls lost ground to line dancing during the early 1990s line-dance boom, then rebounded in the 2000s and 2010s with a new generation of dancers.
Nanci Griffith grew up in Austin's Rollingwood neighborhood and recorded her debut album There's a Light Beyond These Woods in Austin in 1977–78.
"Red Headed Stranger" was a ballad Willie Nelson had used as a lullaby for his kids and played on his DJ shows for years before recording the 1975 concept album.

Swiss Alp Hall, about 11 miles south of La Grange in Fayette County, was built around 1901 by German immigrant Charles Bruns — its stage has hosted Bob Wills, Adolph Hofner, Johnny Winter, and B.J. Thomas.
"Duck under" is a standard move across multiple partner-dance traditions (contra, swing, country) where one or both partners pass beneath an arched connection.
1923 (Paramount+) is a Yellowstone prequel set during Prohibition and the Great Depression, starring Harrison Ford and Helen Mirren.
Doug Sahm, of the Sir Douglas Quintet, moved from San Antonio to Austin in 1972 and became a defining figure of the city's cross-genre music scene.
West Coast Swing entered Texas country-dance venues in the 1980s and 90s — largely through the UCWDC country-dance competition circuit — not in the 1950s as sometimes claimed.
The Chicks (formerly Dixie Chicks) formed in Dallas in 1989; Natalie Maines's father Lloyd Maines is a legendary Texas producer and steel guitarist now based in Austin.
"On the Road Again" was written on an airsickness bag during a 1980 flight when the Honeysuckle Rose production team asked Willie for a road song mid-movie.
Most traditional Hill Country dance halls have no A/C — hinged side flaps open to let breezes through, with swamp coolers common in summer.

The word "waltz" comes from the German "walzen" ("to roll, turn, revolve"). The dance evolved from the rural Ländler in late-18th-century Austria — a 1797 German pamphlet decried it as "the Main Source of Weakness of the Body and Mind of our Generation."
The Last Picture Show (1971) was filmed in Archer City, TX — the hometown of author Larry McMurtry, who co-wrote the screenplay.
Hole in the Wall on Guadalupe opened on June 15, 1974, and has hosted UT-area bands for five decades.
The Nightclub Two-Step is a 4-count "quick-quick-slow" pattern — a different dance from the 4-count Texas two-step, and easy to confuse.
Blaze Foley, the beloved Austin folk singer, was shot and killed on February 1, 1989; Lucinda Williams wrote "Drunken Angel" as a tribute on Car Wheels on a Gravel Road (1998).
Townes Van Zandt said of "Pancho and Lefty": "I kinda always knew it wasn't really about Pancho Villa" — Pancho in the song is hanged, while Villa was assassinated.
The Texas Dance Hall Preservation nonprofit was founded in 2007 by Stephen Dean, Steph McDougal, and Patrick Sparks to save the state's historic halls from demolition.

East Coast Swing was not a grassroots dance — Arthur Murray studios created it in the 1940s as a simplified, standardized alternative to Lindy Hop for beginners. It was originally called "Eastern Swing."
Lonesome Dove (1989 miniseries) was adapted from Larry McMurtry's 1985 Pulitzer-winning novel and starred Robert Duvall and Tommy Lee Jones.
Stubb's BBQ & Music opened in Austin in 1996 at 801 Red River, named for C.B. "Stubb" Stubblefield, who had died the year before.
"East Coast Swing" is often danced to faster country tunes that are too quick for a comfortable two-step — "country swing" is a recognized hybrid.
Doug Sahm was a San Antonio child prodigy on steel guitar — he appeared on KMAC radio, played the Louisiana Hayride as "Little Doug," and shared stages with Hank Williams.
"Waltz Across Texas" was written by Ernest Tubb's nephew, Quanah Talmadge Tubb (who performed as "Billy Talmadge"), and released in 1965.
Sefcik Hall (1923) in Seaton, TX, was built by Tomas and Terezia Sefcik and is still run by their grandchildren — the third generation of the family.
"Traveling" full-floor progressive dancing is hardest on crowded floors — experienced social dancers compress into slot-style or side-by-side holds to avoid collisions.
Sweet Dreams (1985), the Patsy Cline biopic starring Jessica Lange, filmed its dance-hall scenes in Martinsburg, West Virginia — standing in for Virginia.
Stevie Ray Vaughan grew up in Oak Cliff, Dallas, and moved to Austin in 1972 — rising through the club scene with Paul Ray and the Cobras (1975) and Triple Threat (1977).
The Texas Tommy is a swing-out pattern that originated in early-1900s San Francisco (Johnny Peters, c. 1909) and seeded the Lindy Hop swing-out — not the other way around.
Kinky Friedman, of Kinky Friedman & the Texas Jewboys, ran as an independent for Texas governor in 2006 and finished fourth with 12.4% of the vote.
"San Antonio Rose" (1938 instrumental, 1940 lyrics as "New San Antonio Rose") was Bob Wills's signature theme for 40 years; its melody derives from Wills's earlier "Spanish Two Step."
Austin Saengerrunde (organized February 8, 1879) operates attached to Scholz Garten, which was founded by August Scholz in 1866 — together among Austin's oldest continuous businesses.

"Boot Scootin' Boogie" by Brooks & Dunn (released May 25, 1992) hit #1 country that August and is credited with sparking the 1990s line-dance revival — though Asleep at the Wheel had recorded it first in 1990.
Walk the Line (2005), about Johnny Cash, was filmed in Arkansas, Tennessee, and Mississippi — chronicling Cash's Arkansas youth and his rise through Sun Records in Memphis.
Janis Joplin enrolled at UT Austin in summer 1962 and left in January 1963 for San Francisco — making her time as a Texas music student just over six months.
The United Country Western Dance Council (UCWDC) was founded in November 1989 when 21 country/western dance-event directors met in Grantville, Pennsylvania to unify competition rules.
Asleep at the Wheel formed in Paw Paw, West Virginia in 1970 under Ray Benson, moved to the Bay Area, then relocated to Austin in 1974 at Willie Nelson's invitation.

"Good Hearted Woman" was co-written by Waylon Jennings and Willie Nelson in 1969 during a poker game at the Fort Worther Motel in Fort Worth — the famed Willie & Waylon duet version is from the 1976 Wanted! The Outlaws album.
Gruene Hall's stage has hosted George Strait (his first gig on February 21, 1976), Willie Nelson, Garth Brooks (2019 Dive Bar Tour), and Ernest Tubb — the first Nashville-based artist to play there.
A "drop" (lowering the follower close to the floor) is explicitly discouraged on social dance floors — social-dance etiquette guides advise against drops and aerials outside performance contexts.
Willie Nelson has appeared in 30+ films — Honeysuckle Rose (1980), Barbarosa (1982), and Wag the Dog (1997) are often cited among his most notable roles.

Donn's Depot took its name in 1978 when Donn Adelman took over the McNeil Depot Saloon (opened 1972); the building is an 1880s-era Missouri-Pacific train depot moved from McNeil to Austin in 1970. Adelman was resident pianist for decades.
The Nightclub Two-Step was invented in 1965 by teenager Buddy Schwimmer in Whittier, California — designed for slow ballad tempos and often confused with the Texas two-step.
Robert Earl Keen and Lyle Lovett met as Texas A&M classmates in the late 1970s — becoming friends on Keen's College Station front porch, where they co-wrote "The Front Porch Song" / "This Old Porch."
"Me and Bobby McGee," Kris Kristofferson's song, topped the Billboard Hot 100 only after Janis Joplin's posthumous 1971 recording — the second posthumous #1 in U.S. chart history.

The Stafford Opera House in Columbus, TX (1886), built by cattleman Robert E. Stafford, was designed by Galveston architect Nicholas J. Clayton in the Second Empire style — its first performance starred Lillian Russell.
The "triple step" variation (substituting triple steps for slows) is a common two-step alternative for faster songs — its rhythm derives from East Coast Swing rather than any single Texas origin.
Crazy Heart (2009) earned Jeff Bridges the Best Actor Oscar for playing broken-down country singer Bad Blake — the film also won Best Original Song for "The Weary Kind."
